questions.question: What problem with on-policy A2C does PPO solve?
options: A2C can't use neural networks

After one gradient update the policy changes, making the collected rollout off-policy — A2C must throw expensive data away after a single use; PPO's clipped importance ratio allows 10+ epochs on the same rollout without the policy exploding
A2C requires a replay buffer
A2C only works on discrete actions
questions.explanation: TRPO solved it first with a hard KL trust-region constraint, but needed a conjugate-gradient solve per update. PPO got good-enough guarantees with one extra line of code — still the 2026 default.

questions.question: What is the importance ratio r_t(θ)?
options: The reward divided by the value
π_θ(a_t|s_t) / π_θold(a_t|s_t) — the likelihood ratio of the new policy vs the one that collected the data; r=1 means no change, r=2 means the action is now twice as likely
The fraction of clipped gradients
The advantage divided by its std
questions.explanation: Computed in practice as exp(log π_θ − log π_old). It's what lets stale data give an unbiased-ish gradient — until the ratio drifts too far, which is what clipping polices.

questions.question: How does the clipped surrogate L^CLIP behave?
options: It clips rewards to [−1, 1]

min(r·A, clip(r, 1−ε, 1+ε)·A): with A>0 the gradient flattens once r exceeds 1+ε (don't over-push good actions); with A<0 it caps at 1−ε (don't over-suppress); the min ensures no clipping on the side that would hurt you
It zeroes all negative advantages
It normalizes the policy entropy
questions.explanation: Plotted against r, it's piecewise-linear with a flat roof on the good side and flat floor on the bad side. Typical ε = 0.2.

questions.question: What does one PPO training iteration look like?
options: One env step, one gradient step

Collect N×T transitions across parallel envs, compute GAE advantages and FREEZE them, snapshot π_old, then run K epochs of minibatch updates using the clipped objective with ratios computed against the snapshot
Train the critic to convergence first
Replay random transitions like DQN
questions.explanation: Freezing advantages and the old policy is what makes multi-epoch reuse coherent — the ratio measures total drift from the data-collecting policy, not from the last minibatch.

questions.question: Why did PPO outlive TRPO?
options: TRPO was mathematically wrong

TRPO's hard KL constraint needs a conjugate-gradient solve per update; PPO's clipping achieves a similar trust-region effect with a one-line objective change, ten epochs per rollout, and no second-order machinery — simplicity won
PPO has better theoretical guarantees
TRPO was never open-sourced
questions.explanation: Nine years on, PPO remains the default policy-gradient algorithm from MuJoCo to RLHF — the canonical case of "good-enough theory plus excellent engineering ergonomics."
